title: A1 and A2 v. W1 and W2 (Hong Kong, Court of First Instance, In the High Court of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region)
text:
A A
B
B
HCCT 14/2022
C
[2022] HKCFI 3173 C
[amended and redacted copy]
D
D
E IN THE HIGH COURT OF THE
E
HONG KONG SPECIAL ADMINISTRATIVE REGION
F
F
COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE
G CONSTRUCTION AND ARBITRATION PROCEEDINGS
G
NO 14 OF 2022
H
H
____________________
I
I
IN THE MATTER OF Section 87 of
the Arbitration Ordinance (Cap 609)
J
J
and
K
K
IN THE MATTER OF arbitration
L awards obtained by A1 and A2 against
L
W1 and W2 in LCIA Arbitration Case
M No. 153051 dated 12 March 2019 and
M
13 October 2021 respectively
N
____________________ N
O BETWEEN
O
A1 1st Plaintiff
P
P
A2 2nd Plaintiff
Q
Q
and
R
R
W1 1st Defendant
S
S
W2 2nd Defendant
T
____________________ T
U
U
V
V

- 2 -
A A
Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers
B
B
Dates of Written Submissions: 8, 22 and 27 September 2022
C Date of Decision: 17 October 2022
C
D _____________
D
D E C I S I O N
E
E
_____________
F
F
Background
G
G
1. By their Originating Summons issued on 2 March 2022, the
H
Plaintiffs in these proceedings applied for leave to enforce the partial H
award and the final award made by the Tribunal on 12 March 2019 and
I
I
13 October 2021 respectively (“Awards”) in two co-joined LCIA
J
arbitrations against the Defendants (“Arbitration”). Under the Awards, J
the 2nd Defendant was ordered to pay to the 1st Plaintiff a sum of
K
K
US$142,943,168, and interest; and the Defendants were ordered to jointly
L
and severally pay to the Plaintiffs their total fees and disbursements of L
US$6,185,886.85, and arbitration costs of GBP 618,249.91. By an order of
M
M
9 March 2022, leave was granted on the Originating Summons
N
(“Enforcement Order”). N
O
O
2. On 17 May 2022, the Defendants applied to set aside the
P
Enforcement Order, on the stated grounds that: (1) the Awards were not P
Q awards which the Hong Kong Court would enforce, as they are not
Q
commercial cases under the commercial reservation made by the People’s
R
R
Republic of China (“PRC”) upon accession to the New York Convention,
S as applied to Hong Kong after 1997, and/or that the Awards are not
S
Convention awards within the meaning of the Arbitration Ordinance
T
T
(“Ordinance”) (“Commercial Reservation Ground”); (2) the Awards
U
U
V
V

- 3 -
A A
were made in respect of a matter which is not capable of settlement by
B
B
arbitration under the laws of Hong Kong, in that the Awards constituted
C direct or indirect enforcement of tax and/or revenue laws of the PRC
C
(“Arbitrability Ground”); and (3) enforcement of the Awards would be
D
D
contrary to public policy as it would infringe and/or be contrary to the laws
E
of the PRC (“Public Policy Ground”). E
F
F
3. The Defendants’ setting aside application has been scheduled
G
for hearing in February 2023. G
H
H
4. On 4 July 2022, the Plaintiffs applied for security to be
I
provided by the Defendants, pending the hearing of the Defendants’ setting I
aside application, and in default of security, for the setting aside
J
J
application to be dismissed. The Plaintiffs further seek security for their
K
costs, on the ground that the Defendants are ordinarily out of the K
jurisdiction and that, on their purported case, they have no assets in
L
L
Hong Kong.
M
M
5. The application for security was directed to be dealt with on
N
N
the papers.
O
O
P
Applicable legal principles P
Q 6. The legal principles applicable to determination of an
Q
application for security are not disputed. They are as set out in Soleh
R
R
Boneh International Ltd v Government of the Republic of Uganda [1993] 2
S Lloyd’s Rep 208 at 212, applied in Guo Shun Kai v Wing Shing Chemical
S
Co Ltd [2013] 3 HKLRD 484 and Dana Shipping and Trading SA v Sino
T
T
Channel Asia Ltd [2017] 1 HKC 281, and will not be repeated here.
U
U
V
V

- 4 -
A A
The strength of the argument that the Awards are invalid
B
B
7. The strength of the argument that the award is invalid, as
C
perceived on a brief consideration by the court, is the first important factor C
D to be considered on an application for security. As Staughton LJ explained
D
in Soleh Boneh, if the award is manifestly invalid, there should be an
E
E
adjournment and no order for security, and if it is manifestly valid, there
F should be either an order for immediate enforcement, or else an order for
F
substantial security. In between where there are various degrees of
G
G
plausibility in the argument for invalidity, the court must be guided by its
H preliminary conclusion on the point.
H
I
I
8. The merits of the Defendants’ application are at this stage to
J be perceived on a brief consideration only. On such a brief consideration, I
J
fail to see how it can be said that the Awards dealt with the Tribunal’s
K
K
determination of the tax liability of W2, the 2nd Defendant and Target
L Company which was the subject of the Stock Purchase Agreement (“SPA”)
L
made between the 1st Plaintiff as Seller, the 2nd Plaintiff as the Seller’s
M
M
Guarantor, the 1st Defendant as Buyer and X, the Buyer’s Guarantor. The
N Arbitration related to the parties’ dispute under the SPA, a commercial
N
agreement made between the parties for the sale and purchase of the share
O
O
capital of the 2nd Defendant. The Awards concern the Tribunal’s
P
determination of the rights and liabilities of the parties under their P
commercial relationship created under the SPA, and the decision made by
Q
Q
the Tribunal as to the 1st Defendant’s liability to make the payment to the
R
Plaintiffs was based on the Tribunal’s construction of the SPA, and of the R
indemnity under the relevant clauses of the SPA as to the responsibility of
S
S
the Seller for any taxes of the Target Company attributable to the time
T
prior to and including the Closing Date of the transaction, net any tax T
U
U
V
V

- 5 -
A A
benefit actually realized by the Buyer or the Target Company with respect
B
B
to the taxes.
C
C
9. It was not disputed in the Arbitration that the 1st Plaintiff had
D
D
made a payment of US$193.8 million to the Mainland tax authority, on
E
behalf of the Target Company, on a without prejudice basis, as agreed E
between the parties to the SPA. The tax paid had been determined by the
F
F
relevant Mainland tax authority, and paid pursuant to the demands made
G
by the tax authority on the Target Company. The relevant tax authority G
then confirmed in writing to the Target Company, at its request, that it had
H
H
become entitled to reduced tax liabilities under PRC law. It was on such
I
basis that the Tribunal found that a tax benefit had crystallized upon and as I
a result of the 1st Plaintiff’s payment of the tax assessed, that the Target
J
J
Company had received the benefit, and that there was a causal connection
K
or relation between the tax payment made by the 1st Plaintiff on behalf of K
the Target Company and the receipt by the Target Company of the tax
L
L
benefit. The Tribunal then proceeded to evaluate and assess the value of
M
the tax benefit received and to be accountable and reimbursed by the M
Defendants to the Plaintiffs in accordance with the provisions of the SPA.
N
N
This was done on the basis of the Tribunal’s construction of the terms of
O
the SPA, and in particular the Tribunal’s construction of the 2nd Plaintiff’s O
P liability under the indemnity clause of the SPA for the amount of the tax of
P
the Target Company up to the completion of the transaction, net of any tax
Q
Q
benefit received by the Buyer/ 1st Defendant, or the Target Company/
R 2nd Defendant, on the evidence and basis of what was notified and
R
confirmed by the Mainland tax authority. There was, at the quantum
S
S
hearing, expert evidence on the value of the tax benefit, on the basis of
T which the Tribunal ascertained and determined the value of the tax benefit
T
U
U
V
V

- 6 -
A A
received by the Target Company, to rule on the restitutionary
B
B
compensation to be made by the 2nd Defendant/ the Target Company to the
C 1st Plaintiff under the Final Award on quantum.
C
D
D
10. The PRC expert opinion cited the PRC law which provides
E
that the tax authority has the right to make adjustments or reductions of E
taxable income and the taxes for which the Target Company and/or the
F
F
1st Plaintiff should be liable, and argued that in substance, the Tribunal had
G
adjudicated on the question of whether or not the Target Company was G
entitled to the tax benefit, which is a PRC tax issue. I fail to see how it can
H
H
be said that the Tribunal has deprived the relevant Mainland tax authority
I
of its right to charge or rule on any tax payable, or to relieve a party from I
any liability for tax, by virtue simply of the Awards. The decision of the
J
J
Tribunal on the tax benefit received, and what it was worth, was made by
K
the Tribunal on the basis of the contractual provisions of the SPA which K
governed the rights and liabilities of the parties to the SPA inter se, and on
L
L
the basis of the written confirmation issued by the relevant tax authority
M
confirming the Target Company’s entitlement. There is nothing to suggest M
that if the relevant tax authority considers, for any reason, that the Target
N
N
Company is liable for additional tax, it is precluded by the Awards to look
O
O
to the Target Company, or to the parties to the SPA, for any tax properly
P payable by either the Target Company, or by either the Plaintiffs or the
P
Defendants. I can see no question of the Tribunal usurping the role of the
Q
Q
Mainland tax authorities in adjudicating any tax payable or allocating any
R tax benefit.
R
S
S
11. Needless to say, the Court is not concerned with the
T correctness of the Tribunal’s decision as to the value of the tax benefit,
T
U
U
V
V

- 7 -
A A
its allocation amongst the parties to the SPA under the provisions of the
B
B
SPA, or as to the tax payable by any party or any deductions applicable.
C
C
12. Hence, I am not satisfied that the Awards dealt directly or
D
D
indirectly with foreign revenue, or with enforcement of foreign revenue
E
law. Even if the Commercial Reservation was applicable, I am satisfied at E
this stage that the Awards dealt with the commercial relationship between
F
F
the parties arising under their commercial agreement, the SPA. For that
G
same reason, the Arbitrability Ground and the Public Policy Ground have G
no application.
H
H
I
I
The ease or difficulty of the enforcement of the Awards
J
13. In applying the relevant legal principles to the determination J
of whether to order security as a condition for adjourning the enforcement
K
K
proceedings, the Court is to compare the position of the creditor under the
L
award, if it was allowed to enforce the award immediately, and its position L
if enforcement was to be delayed as a result of an adjournment
M
M
(Soleh Boneh International Ltd v Government of the Republic of Uganda
N
[1993] 2 Lloyd’s Rep 208, IPCO (Nigeria) Ltd v Nigerian National N
O Petroleum Corporation [2005] 1 Lloyd’s Rep 475, and Dana Shipping and
O
Trading SA v Sino Channel Asia Ltd [2017] 1 HKC 281). The focus of the
P
P
exercise is to consider the ease or difficulty of the enforcement of the
Q award, and whether it will be rendered more difficult if enforcement is
Q
delayed, and to ascertain whether there is prejudice to the creditor as a
R
R
result of the adjournment (A v B [2022] HKCFI 607).
S
S
14. In this case, the Defendants put emphasis on the fact that they
T
T
do not have any assets, or (in their terminology) “any meaningful assets”,
U
U
V
V

- 8 -
A A
in Hong Kong. They argue that the Court should not order them to bring
B
B
assets into Hong Kong, when none had previously existed to which the
C Plaintiffs could look for purposes of enforcement of any award in
C
Hong Kong, and that to do so would be improving the position of the
D
D
creditor and unfair to the Defendants (Karaha Bodas Co LLC
E
v Persusahaan Pertambangan Minydak Dan Gas Bumi Negara [2003] 2 E
HKLRD 381).
F
F
G
15. As was pointed out in X v Jemmy Chen [2019] HKCFI 2172, G
if a debtor did not have assets within the jurisdiction of Hong Kong in the
H
H
first place, this is one factor against the grant of security, although it is not
I
the only relevant nor the determining factor, as illustrated in Weili Su I
v Shengkang Fei [2019] 2 HKLRD 1214. The particular facts in
J
J
Karaha Bodas have to be borne in mind, to understand the reason why
K
security was not ordered against the background of all the factors existing K
in the case: of there being existing security against assets in Hong Kong,
L
L
the availability of other disclosed assets elsewhere and there being
M
no evidence of dissipation in Hong Kong. M
N
N
16. I am not satisfied that the Defendants in this case have been
O
O
forthright with regard to their assets, nor that there is no risk of dissipation
P of their assets.
P
Q
Q
17. In their evidence, the Defendants claim that they never had
R any “meaningful assets” within Hong Kong which were available for
R
enforcement. The 2nd Defendant’s assets are its interests under contracts
S
S
for offshore oil and gas exploration and development in the Mainland. The
T 1st Defendant is a special purpose vehicle incorporated in the BVI, and its
T
U
U
V
V

- 9 -
A A
purpose is to hold all the shares in the 2nd Defendant, which is a Bahamian
B
B
company. According to the affirmation of the General Counsel of the
C Defendants, the 1st Defendant has “very limited assets” other than the
C
shares in the 2nd Defendant. It was claimed that other than a bank account
D
D
maintained by the 1st Defendant with CMBC in Hong Kong, it has no other
E
assets, and that the bank account in question holds “minimum cash”. In E
support, the 1st Defendant produced selected bank statements and
F
F
transaction records for April 2022 and May 2022, showing that it had
G
balances of approximately US$600 in the CMBC account. The Defendants G
also produced selected bank transaction records relating to the 2nd
H
H
Defendant’s account with Citibank in Hong Kong, to show that it had
I
balances of approximately US$500 to US$2,300 in the account, and that I
there have been “little usage and movement” in the account, and only
J
J
“minimal amounts” held therein.
K
K
18. It was only when the Plaintiffs’ solicitors wrote to seek
L
L
production and inspection of the full monthly bank statements of the
M
2 bank accounts, for the period from 1 October 2021, that the Defendants M
produced further bank statements. These show that from December 2021,
N
N
the 2nd Defendant had at least received monthly payments of US$600,000
O
O
in the Citibank account, and had on receipt immediately paid out the exact
P amount to an entity referred to as XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
P
(“Investments”).
Q
Q
R 19. Counsel for the Defendants sought to downplay this by
R
claiming that these were of de minimus amounts, compared to the principal
S
S
sum of US$143 million under the Awards. I accept the submissions made
T
T
U
U
V
V

- 10 -
A A
on behalf of the Plaintiffs, that the monthly receipt and payment out of
B
B
US$600,000 can scarcely be said to be of de minimus.
C
C
20. In my view, the Defendants only chose to make partial
D
D
disclosure of their assets in Hong Kong, and have not been forthcoming in
E relation to the payments in and out of the 2nd Defendant’s account of E
US$600,000 per month. I do not agree that these are small amounts which
F
F
do not need to be properly explained, as they do comprise assets of the
G
2nd Defendant in Hong Kong if they had not been paid out or dissipated. G
The Defendants have not adequately explained why the US$600,000 had to
H
H
be paid out from the 2nd Defendant’s account to another entity, Investments,
I
when on the Defendants’ case, they formed part of the funds raised under I
the alleged “sell and leaseback arrangement” with
J
J
XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
K
(“CN”) and were to be used to repay the loans of the 2nd Defendant. The K
Plaintiffs’ case is that these payments out of the 2nd Defendant’s bank
L
L
account in Hong Kong constituted dissipation of assets. On the evidence
M
given by the Defendants, I am not persuaded that there has been sufficient M
and satisfactory explanation given that the funds transferred out of the
N
N
2nd Defendant’s account were made in the ordinary course of the
O
2nd Defendant’s business, or for its ordinary and normal expenses. O
P
P
21. On the Plaintiffs’ case, X which was a listed company and the
Q
Guarantor of the 1st Defendant under the SPA and the parent company of Q
R the 1st Defendant, had become delisted as early as XXXXXXX 2020. In
R
XXXXX 2021, the Hong Kong Stock Exchange (“Exchange”) had
S
S
published a statement in respect of disciplinary proceedings which had
T
T
U
U
V
V

- 11 -
A A
been taken against X and its 4 directors, including Mr XXXX (“Y”), the
B
B
only executive director of X and the sole director of the 2nd Defendant.
C
C
22. The Plaintiffs claim that according to the 2020 Annual Report
D
D
of X which was published on 10 August 2021, the financial status of X and
E
the Defendants has been in a state of deterioration. As at 30 June 2020, the E
X group of companies (“Group”) had defaulted in approximately
F
F
HK$4.554 billion of its total borrowings of HK$9.21 billion. According to
G
an earlier announcement which had been issued by X on XXXXXXX G
2018, the 2nd Defendant had entered into a finance lease agreement with
H
H
CN with a principal amount up to US$406 million. According to the 2020
I
Annual Report, the 2nd Defendant and/or X had defaulted in repayment of I
its borrowings from CN. The ultimate controlling shareholder of X and a
J
J
guarantor of certain debts of the Group, one Dr Z, had been adjudged
K
bankrupt by the Hong Kong Court on XXXXXXXXX . K
L
L
23. According to the 2020 Annual Report, the value of the total
M
assets of the Group, which represented and/or included the assets owned M
by the Defendants, had changed dramatically from HK$5.942 billion
N
N
in 2019 to only HK$498 million in 2020, with a decrease of over 91%.
O
O
P 24. The Plaintiffs highlighted the fact that the 2020 Annual Report
P
of the Group contained a qualified opinion of the auditors, which stated
Q
Q
that they had not been able to obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence
R to provide a basis for an audit opinion on the consolidated financial
R
statements of the Group. According to the auditors, they were unable to
S
S
obtain sufficient audit evidence in relation to (inter alia) the impairment
T assessment of the assets of the Group as at 30 June 2020 and 2019.
T
U
U
V
V

- 12 -
A A
25. On the Plaintiffs’ case, the Defendants are in a dire financial
B
B
situation and further delay in enforcement of the Awards would be
C prejudicial to the Plaintiffs, in that it would become more difficult to
C
recover the amount due to them from the Defendants. This is more
D
D
apparent when the conduct of the Defendants’ officers and of their parent
E
company is taken into consideration. E
F
F
26. On 20 October 2020, X’s listing was cancelled by the
G
Exchange after 2 to 3 years of non-compliance with the Listing Rules and G
the suspension of its trading on the Exchange. There had been
H
H
investigations into suspicious transactions conducted in the business of the
I
Group, which investigations had eventually led to X’s delisting. In the I
Statement of Disciplinary Action issued by the Exchange against X and its
J
J
directors including Y, the Exchange noted that there had been a blatant
K
refusal by the directors to procure X to comply with the repeated requests K
of the Exchange to publish the announcement of X’s delisting, which
L
L
constituted the directors’ willful and persistent failure to discharge their
M
responsibilities under the Listing Rules. The Exchange further stated that M
the retention of the directors’ office would have been prejudicial to the
N
N
interests of investors, had the company remained listed.
O
O
P 27. On behalf of the Defendants, it was argued that they should be
P
kept separate from X, despite the fact that the 1st Defendant is a wholly-
Q
owned subsidiary of X, and the 2nd Defendant is a wholly-owned Q
R subsidiary of the 1st Defendant. They claim that their business of petroleum
R
production is only one of the several lines of X’s business, that the
S
S
Defendants had reputable partners on the Mainland, and that they had or
T shared interests in oilfields on the Mainland. They claim that their business
T
U
U
V
V

- 13 -
A A
had nothing to do with X’s delisting which was more related to the
B
B
international trading and bunkering business led by individuals who were
C not involved in the Defendants’ line of business. The Defendants claim
C
that irrespective of the financial results disclosed in the 2020 Annual
D
D
Report of X, the Defendants remain going concerns and “are likely to
E
continue to do business” (emphasis added). E
F
F
28. In relation to the financial figures, the Defendants claim that
G
the Group’s interests in the oilfields depend on market conditions and on G
crude oil prices, and that these prices have recovered significantly since the
H
H
2020 Annual Report. They seek to place reliance on the draft accounts of
I
X for the year ended 30 June 2021, to claim that the total assets of the I
Group have increased to approximately HK$5 billion for the year ended 30
J
J
June 2021, and that there has been reversal of impairment loss, with a
K
consequent sum of $1,667 million written back into the books of the Group. K
L
L
29. However, as pointed out by the Plaintiffs, these draft accounts
M
of X for the period up to 30 June 2021 have still not been finalized, and are M
unreliable. The Plaintiffs highlighted that X’s accounts for 2020 had been
N
N
subject to the auditors’ qualified opinion, and that the Defendants have
O
O
failed to explain why their accounts up to June 2021 could not be finalized
P as at August 2022. In any event, the Plaintiffs highlighted the Defendants’
P
acceptance that the value of their assets and interests in the oil fields are
Q
Q
subject to severe and rapid fluctuation.
R
R
30. I do not accept the Defendants’ attempts to play down the
S
S
censure or disciplinary proceedings against Y, who is the executive
T director of X and also the sole director and controller of the 2nd Defendant.
T
U
U
V
V

- 14 -
A A
There are high standards expected of directors of listed companies, who
B
B
should be trusted, and must be seen, to act in compliance with the Listing
C Rules and the obligations imposed thereunder on a listed company and its
C
directors. If Y does not act in accordance with these high standards and
D
D
cannot comply even with basic demands made by the regulators, it reflects
E
very badly on his ability and intentions to comply with orders of the Court. E
If Y had no hesitation in flouting the Listing Rules, and in ignoring the
F
F
repeated demands made by the regulators with regard to compliance, as the
G
evidence has shown, he simply cannot be trusted with observing orders of G
the Court and of the Tribunal. He is just as likely to consider that the
H
H
Defendants which he controls can be excused from complying with their
I
obligations under the Awards to make payment, and can do what they like I
with their assets despite any Award or judgment made against the
J
J
Defendants, or to find reasons to evade the Defendants’ liabilities, pending
K
appeals, or pending further hearings. That was the position taken by Y, K
when he and X were directed by the Exchange to make the announcement
L
L
of X’s delisting, on the excuse and purported basis that X was still
M
challenging the decision of the Exchange. M
N
N
31. There is an interim of 9 months between the issue of the
O
O
summons to set aside the Enforcement Order and the adjourned hearing of
P the application, and an interim of 7 months between the issue of the
P
summons for security and the adjourned hearing in February 2023.
Q
Q
I do not consider that there is undue delay in the Plaintiffs’ application for
R the Enforcement Order from the time when the Awards were made. I bear
R
in mind that any delay in enforcement is against the interests of the
S
S
Plaintiffs which have succeeded in obtaining a final and binding award,
T and further against this Court’s policy of enforcement of arbitration
T
U
U
V
V

- 15 -
A A
agreements and arbitral awards. I have also considered the merits of the
B
B
Defendants’ setting aside application on a preliminary basis, and conclude
C in all the circumstances of this case that it is appropriate to order security
C
to be furnished by the Defendants.
D
D
E
E
Disposition
F
32. I will make orders in terms of paragraphs 1 to 3 of the F
Plaintiffs’ summons of 4 July 2022 (“Summons”), save that the amount of
G
G
security is to be 40% of the amounts specified in the Final Award on
H
Quantum. H
I
I
33. Since the Defendants seeking to set aside the Enforcement
J
Order are clearly outside the jurisdiction of Hong Kong, there is also good J
ground on the evidence to order them to provide security for costs, which
K
K
on broad-brush basis, I will order in the sum of HK$750,000. Orders will
L
be made in terms of paragraphs 4 and 5 of the Summons, with L
HK$750,000 substituting HK$1,100,000.
M
M
N
34. The costs of the Summons are to be paid by the Defendants to N
O the Plaintiffs, to be summarily assessed if not agreed. The Plaintiffs are at
O
liberty to file and serve their Statement of Costs within 7 days and the
P
P
Defendants are at liberty to file and serve their Statement of Objection
Q within 7 days thereafter.
Q
R
R
S
S
(Mimmie Chan)
T Judge of the Court of First Instance
T
High Court
U
U
V
V

- 16 -
A A
B
The 1st & 2nd plaintiffs were represented by Kobre & Kim B
C Mr Jose Maurellet SC and Mr Alexander Tang, instructed by Li & Partners,
C
for the 1st & 2nd defendants
D
D
E
E
F
F
G
G
H
H
I
I
J
J
K
K
L
L
M
M
N
N
O
O
P
P
Q
Q
R
R
S
S
T
T
U
U
V
V

parties: A1 and A2 v. W1 and W2
